title: Agreement between cognitive screening tools: a comparison of MoCA, ACE-III, M-ACE, and MoCA-MIS
abstract:
Introduction: Early detection of cognitive decline is critical, yet commonly used screening tools may yield divergent results. This study examined the alignment of four widely used instruments in a community-dwelling cohort.

Method: Seventy-nine participants (Mage = 71.20, SD = 6.29; 39 male, 40 female) were recruited from the general community and assessed under standardized laboratory conditions. Participants completed the Montreal Cognitive Assessment (MoCA) and the Addenbrooke’s Cognitive Examination-III (ACE-III), from which scores for the MoCA Memory Index Score (MoCA-MIS) and the Mini-Addenbrooke’s Cognitive Examination (M-ACE) were also calculated. Test agreement was examined using observed agreement and Cohen’s kappa, and score relationships were evaluated using Pearson correlations.

Results: All but one test pair (M-ACE vs MoCA-MIS) showed strong score correlations. However, agreement between MoCA and ACE-III for classifying participants as below threshold was only fair. MoCA identified twice as many participants as impaired compared with ACE-III, raising the possibility of either greater sensitivity or higher false-positive classification. Overall, 41% of participants (n = 32) scored below threshold on at least one measure, but only 6% (n = 5) were below threshold across all four. Of four participants with a prior mild cognitive impairment (MCI) diagnosis, only one, who had more pronounced deficits, scored below threshold in all tests.

Conclusions: Despite strong inter-test correlations, substantial divergence in classification outcome was observed, particularly between MoCA and ACE-III. Reliance on a single screening instrument risks misclassification, with implications for both over- and under-diagnosis. Greater harmonization between screening tools is needed to improve diagnostic consistency. Clinical practice should therefore combine multiple-instrument approaches, potentially including premorbid function, and interpret screening test scores cautiously. Comprehensive neuropsychological assessment may provide a more complete picture, particularly in cases where diagnostic confidence is low, although this is not routine practice in all services.

ABSTRACT
Introduction: Early detection of cognitive decline is critical, yet commonly used screening tools 
may yield divergent results. This study examined the alignment of four widely used instruments in 
a community-dwelling cohort.
Method: Seventy-nine participants (Mage = 71.20, SD = 6.29; 39 male, 40 female) were recruited 
from the general community and assessed under standardized laboratory conditions. Participants 
completed the Montreal Cognitive Assessment (MoCA) and the Addenbrooke’s Cognitive 
Examination-III (ACE-III), from which scores for the MoCA Memory Index Score (MoCA-MIS) and 
the Mini-Addenbrooke’s Cognitive Examination (M-ACE) were also calculated. Test agreement was 
examined using observed agreement and Cohen’s kappa, and score relationships were evaluated 
using Pearson correlations.
Results: All but one test pair (M-ACE vs MoCA-MIS) showed strong score correlations. However, 
agreement between MoCA and ACE-III for classifying participants as below threshold was only fair. 
MoCA identified twice as many participants as impaired compared with ACE-III, raising the 
possibility of either greater sensitivity or higher false-positive classification. Overall, 41% of parti­
cipants (n = 32) scored below threshold on at least one measure, but only 6% (n = 5) were below 
threshold across all four. Of four participants with a prior mild cognitive impairment (MCI) 
diagnosis, only one, who had more pronounced deficits, scored below threshold in all tests.
Conclusions: Despite strong inter-test correlations, substantial divergence in classification out­
come was observed, particularly between MoCA and ACE-III. Reliance on a single screening 
instrument risks misclassification, with implications for both over- and under-diagnosis. Greater 
harmonization between screening tools is needed to improve diagnostic consistency. Clinical 
practice should therefore combine multiple-instrument approaches, potentially including premor­
bid function, and interpret screening test scores cautiously. Comprehensive neuropsychological 
assessment may provide a more complete picture, particularly in cases where diagnostic con­
fidence is low, although this is not routine practice in all services.

Introduction
Cognitive decline, particularly in older adults, can sig­
nificantly impact quality of life and is often a precursor 
to more severe conditions such as dementia. Early 
detection is crucial; cognitive screening tools such as 
the Montreal Cognitive Assessment (MoCA) and the 
Addenbrooke’s Cognitive Examination-III (ACE-III) 
are widely used for this purpose.
The MoCA (Nasreddine et al., 2005) is a brief (10­
15 minute) screening tool which, compared to earlier 
tools, is widely held to be highly sensitive to Mild 
Cognitive Impairment (MCI), a condition characterized 
by a subtle but noticeable decline in memory and 
thinking skills. MoCA was developed to overcome sen­
sitivity limitations and ceiling effects in the Mini-Mental 
State Examination (MMSE; Folstein et al., 1975), which 
has subsequently been found to be less accurate than 
both MoCA and ACE-III (Ciesielska et al., 2016; Giebel 
& Challis, 2017; Jongsiriyanyong & Limpawattana,  
2018; Senda et al., 2020). MoCA assesses multiple cog­
nitive domains, including attention, visuospatial/execu­
tive function, naming, memory, language, abstraction, 
delayed recall, and orientation. The test has a maximum 
score of 30, with scores 26–30 indicating normal cogni­
tion, 18–25 indicating possible MCI, 10–17 indicating

The sensitivity of MoCA may come at the expense of 
specificity, leading some to suggest that it may over- 
identify individuals as cognitively impaired (Luis et al.,  
2009). To compensate for this, it is recommended that 
factors such as education, cultural background, and 
language proficiency are considered when interpreting 
scores. For instance, a score adjustment of + 1 for ≤12  
years of education has been suggested (Nasreddine et al.,  
2005).
A supplemental memory-focused metric, the 
Memory Index Score (MoCA-MIS), can also be calcu­
lated, based on performance in the Delayed Recall 
task. The examiner reads 5 words aloud, for which 
memory is tested following an ~5-minute interval. 
All words that are recalled without cues being pro­
vided score 3 points, giving a maximum of 15 points. 
If a word cannot be recalled, first a semantic cue is 
given (i.e., “it is a body part” for the word “face”). If 
this cue elicits a correct response, 2 points are 
awarded. If the semantic cue does not facilitate retrie­
val, a three-alternative multiple-choice cue is provided. 
If this elicits a correct response, 1 point is awarded. If 
a word is not recalled following both cue types, 0 
points are awarded. The MoCA-MIS score includes 
cued responses, but only un-cued recall contributes 
to the full MoCA score (up to 5 points), so despite 
the Delayed Recall Task being part of the main test, 
MoCA-MIS does capture additional information. 
Although there is no standard guidance for MoCA- 
MIS score interpretation (for instance, a threshold for 
normal memory function), Kaur et al. (2018) reported 
mean scores of 12.2 (SD = 2.8) for normal controls and 
7.8 (SD = 3.8) for participants with amnestic MCI, the 
MCI subtype in which memory is the main cognitive 
domain affected. Similarly, a MoCA-MIS score < 7 has 
been suggested as an indication for individuals with 
MCI to progress to AD (Julayanont et al., 2014).
The ACE-III is more comprehensive than MoCA, 
taking approximately 15–20 minutes to administer 
(Hsieh et al., 2013), and has a maximum score of 100. 
Its more detailed nature may account for reduced sen­
sitivity relative to the MoCA in some contexts (for 
instance, when the deficit is limited to a smaller number 
of cognitive domains). A validation study by Hsieh et al. 
(2013) indicated that the cutoff scores established for 
the earlier ACE-R (88/100 and 82/100) remain appro­
priate for identifying performance below the threshold 
for normal cognition, with 88 being the most widely 
used and scores below 82 suggestive of more serious 
impairment. ACE-III also provides sub-domain scores 
useful for differential diagnosis: attention (/18), memory 
(/26), fluency (/14), language (/26) and visuospatial 
skills (/16). As with MoCA, when interpreting ACE-III 
test scores, it has been suggested that years of education, 
age, and premorbid IQ should be considered (Bruno & 
Schurmann Vignaga, 2019).
An abbreviated variant called M-ACE (mini-ACE) 
also exists that uses a subset of ACE-III questions 
(although unlike the MoCA-MIS, it does not capture 
any information that is not present in the full test). 
M-ACE has a maximum score of 30 and takes approxi­
mately 5 minutes to administer. It has been suggested 
that those with normal cognition should score ≥25 
(Hsieh et al., 2015), and that a score <22 may indicate 
dementia (Bruno & Schurmann Vignaga, 2019). 
M-ACE is sometimes used to decide if a full ACE-III 
should be run, particularly if a near-threshold score is 
obtained, or if the patient subsequently describes symp­
toms in cognitive domains not examined in detail in the 
M-ACE.
While MoCA, MoCA-MIS, ACE-III and M-ACE are 
all effective screening instruments, they can serve com­
plementary roles. The MoCA is particularly useful for 
rapid screening of early cognitive impairment, whereas 
the more comprehensive ACE-III can also be valuable 
for detailed cognitive profiling and differential diagno­
sis. The MoCA-MIS can be used to examine memory 
function more specifically. The M-ACE, a short test 
often used to decide whether a full ACE-III should be 
run, can be interpreted independently in some circum­
stances. Despite this it is common for just one test to be 
used in a particular clinical setting, highlighting the 
need for their periodic comparison.
While individual test characteristics are well- 
documented, their relative performance is a subject of 
debate. Table 1 summarizes findings from key compara­
tive studies. There is no universal agreement as to the 
superior test, or even the optimum threshold values for 
MCI and dementia screening. The words “cut-off” and 
“threshold” are also used inconsistently, and it is some­
times unclear whether proposed thresholds are intended 
to be inclusive (e.g., ≤ x) or exclusive (e.g., < x).
Cheung et al. (2015) conducted a study in New 
Zealand with 84 participants, including 37 diagnosed 
with mild dementia and 47 controls, and reported that 
both MoCA and ACE-III performed well in discrimi­
nating mild dementia from the control group. Using 
their published results, we note that ACE-III had 
a slight advantage in terms of both sensitivity (the pro­
portion of correctly identified true positives) and speci­
ficity (the proportion of correctly identified true 
negatives) individually, and in a combined metric 
(balanced accuracy, the arithmetic mean of sensitivity 
and specificity). Using a small cohort (n = 33), all with

not more than six months prior, Giebel and Challis 
(2017) assessed the sensitivity of MMSE, MoCA, and 
ACE-III relative to measures of everyday activity, con­
cluding ACE-III to be superior in identifying the every­
day functional impairments that differentiate mild and 
moderate dementia. Matias-Guiu, Cortés-Martínez, 
et al. (2017) assessed 200 participants (25 healthy con­

an effective screening tool for detecting early-stage AD 
vs normal, with slightly lower performance in discrimi­
nating MCI vs early-stage AD. In another study from 
the same authors, the diagnostic accuracy of ACE-III, 
M-ACE (extracted from ACE-III post-hoc), Memory 
Impairment 
Screen 
(MIS), 
Mini-Mental 
State 
Examination (MMSE), MoCA, and RUDAS (Rowland 
Universal Dementia Assessment Scale; Storey et al.,  
2004) for AD screening were compared (Matías-Guiu, 
Valles-Salgado et al., 2017). Using 160 participants, 92 
diagnosed with mild AD and 68 healthy controls, 
among all tools examined, M-ACE was found to yield 
the highest specificity and balanced accuracy in 
mild AD detection, while ACE-III had higher 
sensitivity.
Peixoto et al. (2018) found the Portuguese version of 
the ACE-III to have better specificity and sensitivity 
overall compared to the MoCA when differentiating 
healthy controls, MCI, and mild dementia in a sample 
of 90 participants (30 controls, 30 MCI, and 30 with 
dementia), except that sensitivity was found to be higher 
in detecting mild dementia with MoCA. Li et al. (2019) 
examined the Chinese version of ACE-III for the detec­
tion of MCI and mild dementia, comparing it to the 
MMSE and MoCA. Using 176 participants (64 with 
MCI, 57 with mild dementia, and 55 controls) they 
found ACE-III to yield the higher sensitivity and speci­
ficity in MCI detection. Using the values reported, ACE- 
III is also seen to yield higher balanced accuracy than 
MoCA in mild dementia detection, although greater 
specificity for MoCA was reported.
Wang et al. (2019) compared the Chinese versions of 
MoCA and ACE-III with 256 participants (120 with 
MCI and 136 healthy controls) and report no significant 
difference in diagnostic performance, with greater sen­
sitivity for ACE-III but greater specificity for MoCA 
(although calculating balanced accuracy indicates 
a slight advantage for ACE-III). Senda et al. (2020) 
assessed 249 participants (50 controls, 94 with MCI, 
and 105 with dementia) with a range of screening tools 
including ACE-III, M-ACE, and MoCA. They report 
superior performance for ACE-III and M-ACE in both 
MCI and dementia diagnosis, although the values 
reported also indicate greater sensitivity for MoCA in 
MCI detection. Valles-Salgado et al. (2024) compared 
tests including MoCA, ACE-III, and M-ACE with 140 
participants (84 controls, 56 MCI). ACE-III and M-ACE 
were found to have higher specificity and sensitivity 
than MoCA for MCI detection. They suggest that 
M-ACE offers a good balance between performance 
and administration time. Calculating balanced accuracy 
also shows M-ACE to perform best overall, despite 
marginally higher sensitivity for ACE-III. A study by 
Grasina et al. (2024) with 196 participants (71 controls, 
and 125 with cognitive impairment in long-term care), 
also found M-ACE, in its Portuguese translation, to be 
a valid and reliable screening tool to discriminate 
between controls and those with cognitive impairment 
with no dementia (CIND).
Hsieh et al. (2013) concluded that the ACE-III is 
a good screening tool to detect AD using 86 participants 
(61 with dementia and 25 healthy controls). Jubb and 
Evans (2015) assessed the diagnostic accuracy of ACE- 
III for dementia detection using 59 participants and 
proposed a lower score threshold of < 81, suggesting 
that this provided improved diagnostic performance, 
although reported sensitivity is lower than the value 
report in Hsieh et al. (2013). McCarthy et al. (2024) 
also proposed lower threshold scores for ACE-III and 
M-ACE for mild dementia detection (ACE-III ≤76; 
M-ACE ≤19) using 80 participants with dementia, 
observing slightly better performance, expressed as 
AUC, for ACE-III. Performance for moderate dementia 
was very low with both tests, with surprisingly poor 
AUC scores.
Several meta-analyses have compared these screening 
instruments. Larner and Mitchell (2014) examined the 
accuracy of the ACE, ACE-R, and MMSE in detecting 
dementia. Examining numerous studies with varying 
sample sizes and population types, it was concluded 
that ACE-R (the second revision of this test) had super­
ior performance in dementia detection. A meta-analysis 
by Tsoi et al. (2015) suggested that, besides the MMSE, 
the MoCA was best at detecting MCI, and that ACE-R 
and the Mini-Cog (Borson et al., 2000) were best for 
dementia detection from among eleven screening tests 
compared. In contrast, a meta-analysis by Ciesielska 
et al. (2016) compared studies using the MoCA and 
MMSE for MCI detection in those aged over 60, sug­
gesting MoCA to be superior in MCI screening. 
A systematic review by Abd Razak et al. (2019) found 
the MoCA for MCI and ACE-III for dementia to be the 
most accurate screening tools in a primary care setting. 
Davis et al. (2015), in a Cochrane review of the MoCA, 
reported high sensitivity (0.94) but modest specificity

(0.60), concluding that its use cannot be recommended 
as the primary diagnostic tool on the available evidence. 
They highlighted the need for studies that avoid case- 
control designs and instead apply diagnostic tests and 
reference standards prospectively. In a subsequent 
Cochrane review, Beishon et al. (2019) highlight a lack 
of sufficient high-quality data in primary care settings to 
support the use of ACE-III or M-ACE as the primary 
tool for dementia or MCI screening, and advocate for 
the use of full clinical evaluation.
Using the metric of balanced accuracy (calculated by 
us), a holistic measure that takes the average of specifi­
city and sensitivity, many of the above studies found 
ACE-III to be superior in detecting mild dementia 
(Cheung et al., 2015; Li et al., 2019; McCarthy et al.,  
2024; Peixoto et al., 2018; Senda et al., 2020), with one 
study giving superior performance for the abbreviated 
M-ACE (Matías-Guiu, Valles-Salgado, et al., 2017). 
Even when authors suggest that tests elicit broadly the 
same performance, numerically, we find a similar out­
come for MCI, with balanced accuracy being higher for 
ACE-III (Peixoto et al., 2018; Li et al., 2019; Senda et al.,  
2020; Wang et al., 2019), with a different study finding 
superior performance for the M-ACE (Valles-Salgado 
et al., 2024). Despite this, some reviews have suggested 
superior performance for the MoCA (Abd Razak et al.,  
2019; Tsoi et al., 2015). The story is less clear when we 
look at sensitivity and specificity separately, where 
MoCA has been found to yield greater values than ACE- 
III in some cases. For mild dementia, Peixoto et al. 
(2018) report higher sensitivity for MoCA vs ACE-III, 
and Li et al. (2019) report higher specificity. For MCI, 
Wang et al. (2019) report higher specificity for MoCA vs 
ACE-III, and Senda et al. (2020) report higher 
sensitivity.
All studies summarized in Table 1 recruited 
patients through memory clinics, neurology depart­
ments, or used clinical databases of patients with 
existing diagnoses, except one study that included 
participants from long term care facilities (Grasina 
et al., 2024). These clinical studies include those who 
have already contacted health services for assess­
ment, at which point their cognitive decline is likely 
to be more advanced, but do not include those at an 
earlier stage, resulting in a potentially non- 
representative sample. Unlike prior studies which 
relied on clinical samples, often with confirmed 
diagnoses and possibly more advanced cognitive 
decline, our community-based sample may better 
represent individuals at earlier stages, including 
those with undiagnosed MCI, at whom these screen­
ing tests are really directed. Specifically, in existing 
studies, if a participant scored below the threshold of 
normal cognition without a diagnosis they were con­
sidered a “false positive.” In contrast, in our study, if 
someone scored below the threshold without 
a previous formal diagnosis of MCI, given the pre­
valence of undiagnosed MCI in the community 
(Davis et al., 2015; Liu et al., 2024), rather than 
assuming diagnostic error, we remained agnostic as 
to the validity of this outcome.
Administering these tests to the same participant 
group enables their diagnostic utility to be evaluated, 
for instance enabling their degree of agreement (or 
lack thereof) to be observed, which may contribute 
to helping clinicians determine which instrument is 
more appropriate (Carson et al., 2012), or whether 
multiple instruments should be used. In this study, 
we administered MoCA and ACE-III (including cal­
culating sub-scores MoCA-MIS and M-ACE) to 
a cohort of older volunteers in which a surprising 
41% exhibited below-threshold cognition in at least 
one test using standard thresholds, rather than 
revised thresholds proposed by some of the studies 
summarized in Table 1.
The novelty of this study lies in its use of 
a community-based sample, and its combined use 
of correlation and agreement metrics to examine 
test concordance among four screening tools. Our 
primary aim was to examine consistency between 
MoCA and ACE-III in a community-dwelling sam­
ple, not to estimate diagnostic accuracy against 
a “gold standard.” While in the U.S.A., brief screen­
ers such as the MoCA and ACE-III are often sup­
plemented by a full neuropsychological assessment, 
which may include instruments such as the Clinical 
Dementia Rating (CDR; Morris, 1993), in the UK 
and elsewhere they, together with self-report and 
informant history, typically serve as the primary 
tools for initial dementia diagnosis following referral 
by a general practitioner (GP; equivalent to 
a primary care physician). This practice is consistent 
with NHS-implemented NICE Guideline NG97 
(National Institute for Health and Care Excellence,  
2018). This underscores the importance, particularly 
in countries like the UK, of ensuring that these 
screening measures are well aligned (as they are 
sometimes used interchangeably) and are judiciously 
interpreted.
Materials and methods
Participants
A total of 79 older participants were recruited, mean 
age = 71.20 (SD = 6.29), age range 53–82, 39 male and 40

female. All participants had English as their first lan­
guage to help ensure that test instructions were under­
standable. The mean years of education was 16.75 (SD 
3.51). University ethics approval was obtained, and the 
study followed the guidelines of the Declaration of 
Helsinki. Participants were recruited from the Join 
Dementia Research (JDR) volunteer database managed 
by the NIHR (National Institute of Health Research) [n  
= 64], the University of The Third Age (U3A) [n = 6], 
advertising by posters and flyers displayed at the uni­
versity campus [n = 7], and a local retirement home [n  
= 2]. All participants had normal or corrected-to- 
normal vision and hearing. Exclusion criteria included 
the following self-reported conditions: low vision, 
known history of neurological or psychological disor­
ders (excluding MCI), brain injury, epilepsy, dyslexia, 
stroke, degenerative diseases such as Parkinson’s, 
depression, anxiety and chronic alcohol and drug use. 
Four participants presented with a preexisting clinical 
diagnosis of MCI. These participants, although small in 
number, were included so that we did not actively 
exclude cases near the decision boundary or reduce 
ecological validity.
Procedure
Participants completed the MoCA version 8.1 
(Nasreddine et al., 2005), and ACE-III (Hsieh et al.,  
2013) using British English translations, where available. 
MoCA was administered by a certified rater (ID 
GBMCAMO710560679-01). Tests were completed in 
random order in a quiet, well-lit lab without distrac­
tions. All data were collected during a single visit in an 
unhurried manner, and participants were reminded 
regularly that they may take rest breaks when desired. 
Demographic data collected included age and years of 
formal education, which was calculated from the highest 
qualification obtained. To ensure that sensory issues did 
not affect test performance, brief vision and hearing 
assessments were conducted. Normal vision, with cor­
rection if needed, was assessed using MNREAD Acuity 
Chart 2 (Mansfield & Legge, 1990), with all participants 
achieving a minimum visual acuity of LogMAR 0.4. 
Hearing ability, with assistive devices if required, was 
evaluated by having participants listen to five prere­
corded sound clips of color names (e.g., “blue,” 
“black,” “red”) and repeat each word aloud. All partici­
pants repeated all five words correctly. No participants 
were excluded due to impaired vision or hearing.
Analysis
Descriptive statistics (mean and SD) were calculated for 
each test (MoCA, MIS, ACE-III, and M-ACE). Scores 
were interpreted using established criteria, a cutoff of 88 
applied for the ACE-III. The numbers of participants for 
whom test score interpretations were in agreement (e.g., 
scores all indicative of normal cognition, or otherwise) 
vs discrepant (i.e., some scores indicative of normal 
cognition with others not) were examined. Test agree­
ment was evaluated as observed agreement and Cohen’s 
kappa (Ben-David, 2008; McHugh, 2012). Inter-score 
relationships were also evaluated using Pearson product 
moment correlation.
Results
Scores and descriptive statistics for all tests and partici­
pants are summarized in Table 2. The mean raw MoCA 
score was 26.76 (SD 2.50). Thirteen (13) participants 
had ≤12 years of education, so a score adjustment of + 1 
was applied (Nasreddine et al., 2005), however this did 
not have the effect of pushing any participant above the 
normal cognition threshold (i.e., from a score of 25 to 
26). Following this adjustment, the mean MoCA score 
was 26.91 (SD 2.52). From here, education-adjusted 
MoCA scores are used only.
Sixteen (16) participants had a MoCA score < 26 (the 
normal cognition threshold). Fifteen (15) had a score in 
the range 18–25 (indicating MCI), and one (1) had 
a score in the range 10–17 (indicating moderate impair­
ment). Of the four (4) participants with a preexisting 
diagnosis of MCI, three (3) were correctly identified by 
MoCA as falling below the threshold for normal cogni­
tion (score < 26), with scores of 13, 24, and 25. One (1) 
participant with a diagnosis of MCI obtained a score in 
the normal cognition range (27). This means that, of the

Across all participants, the mean ACE-III score was 
93.89 (SD = 6.53). Eight (8) participants had an ACE-III 
score < 88 (the threshold for normal cognition, indicat­
ing possible MCI or more severe impairment), which is 
half as many as those falling below the MoCA threshold 
(10.13% of our sample of older adults, vs 20.25% for 
MoCA). Of these eight (8), one (1) had a score < 61 
(indicative of dementia). Of the four (4) participants 
with a preexisting diagnosis of MCI, two scored below 
the threshold for normal cognition (with scores of 52 
and 86) and two obtained scores in the normal cogni­
tion range (90 and 96). This means that of the eight (8) 
participants that failed to reach the normal cognition 
threshold, six (6) did not have a preexisting MCI diag­
nosis and were also referred to their GP. Of the two 
participants with a preexisting MCI diagnosis that fell 
below the ACE-III normal cognition threshold, only one 
(1) also fell below the normal cognition threshold for 
MoCA. A fuller evaluation of test agreement/disagree­
ment is provided later. Had the more liberal threshold 
for normal cognition of 82/100 been used for ACE-III 
(Hsieh et al., 2013), only two (2) participants would 
have fallen below the threshold (2.53% of our sample), 
and this would have excluded three of the four partici­
pants with an existing MCI diagnosis.
The mean M-ACE score was 27.00 (SD = 3.62) across 
all participants. Eleven (11) participants scored below 
the normal cognition threshold for M-ACE (i.e., < 25) 
and three (3) of these participants scored in the normal 
range for both the ACE-III (≥88) and MoCA (≥26), 
three (3) scored under the normal cognition threshold 
for the ACE-III (< 88) with a MoCA score in the normal 
range (≥26). Five (5) participants scored outside the 
normal range in all three tests (MoCA, ACE-III, and 
M-ACE). Three (3) participants scored < 21 (indicating 
dementia) while their MoCA score was in the normal 
range (≥26). To probe these discrepancies, pairwise 
occurrences of agreement/disagreement were examined.
Across MoCA, MIS, ACE-III, and M-ACE, 32 parti­
cipants (41%) fell below the normal cognition threshold 
in at least one test (union), whilst only five (6%) parti­
cipants fell below the normal threshold in all four tests 
(intersection). Considering only the four scores (MoCA, 
MIS, ACE-III, and M-ACE), there are six possible pair­
wise comparisons in terms of whether the normal cog­
nition threshold was met.
Remaining agnostic as to whether participants were 
truly below the normal cognition threshold, the degree 
of agreement between the tests was calculated by divid­
ing the sum counts in which classifications agreed (YY 
+NN) by the total number of observations (n = 79), 
yielding observed agreement (Po). This value was then 
used to calculate Cohen’s kappa (κ), which evaluates the 
degree to which agreement exceeds that which would be 
expected by chance. These values are presented in 
Table 3, with interpretations suggested by Landis and 
Koch (1977).
As one might expect, given that M-ACE is a sub- 
score of ACE-III that includes several ACE-III ques­
tions, the largest observed agreement (Po = .96) is 
between ACE-III and M-ACE, with the same principle 
also yielding very good agreement between MoCA and 
MoCA-MIS (Po = .80), perhaps slightly lower reflecting 
that MoCA-MIS can contain some information not 
present in MoCA full score. However, overall scores 
for the full tests (MoCA and ACE-III) also show good 
observed agreement at Po = .82, despite twice as many 
participants being identified as below-threshold by 
MoCA compared to ACE-III. However, interpreting 
Cohen’s kappa (κ), gives a slightly less favorable assess­
ment of the relationship between MoCA and ACE-III, 
with only a “fair” agreement (κ = .33), and, as one might

Although it is highly likely that several participants 
without a prior diagnosis of MCI in fact did have MCI,

It is also important to note the number of partici­
pants that scored at threshold, 1 point below or above

classification errors. Here, 34 participants were 
borderline for the MoCA (scores 25–27), 5 were border­
line for ACE-III (scores 87–89), 16 for M-ACE (scores

the more compressed score range M-ACE, MoCA, and 
MoCA-MIS means the number of borderline cases must 
be interpreted cautiously, it does show the potentially 
large impact of corrections such as the education adjust­

earlier, and the vulnerability of shorter tests to inatten­

is classified as “large” (Cohen, 1988). A scatterplot 
showing the correlation between these scores, with

Supplementary Material A shows a correlation 
matrix in which all test scores, sub-scores, and

Our sample scored slightly lower across most of 
the tests compared to available normative data. The 
mean MoCA total score (26.91) is slightly lower than 
the normative data reported by Kenny et al. (2013) 
of 27.2 (SD = 2.2) for aged 70 with primary or no 
education, 28.3 (SD = 1.8) with secondary education, 
and 29.0 (SD = 1.3) with tertiary or higher education. 
Data provided by Kenny et al. (2013) is based on 
representative sample of community-living popula­
tion of Ireland without severe cognitive impairment. 
Comparing with normative data provide by Sachs 
et al. (2022) our sample scored above the 50th per­
centile across all levels of education in both white 
males and females, except white females with grad­
uate degree, in this category our data is only above 
the 25th percentile. Mean MoCA scores were not 
calculated separately for female participants with 
degree-level education in our study. This normative 
data (Sachs et al., 2022) was provided by 
a cognitively normal sample of diverse older U.S. 
adults.
Our mean full ACE-III score (93.89) is lower than 
the mean total score 95.4 (SD = 3.3) of the healthy 
controls provided by Hsieh et al. (2013), although 
the mean age 66.1. (SD = 7.4) of their control group 
was lower compared to our sample. The mean full 
ACE-III score is also lower than the normative data 
from The University of Sydney (2019), which reported 
96 (SD = 2.7) for controls with the mean age of 68.7 
(SD = 7), or data provided by So et al. (2018) for 
healthy controls in which the mean ACE-III score 
was 95.7 (SD = 3.3) with participants of mean age 
69.6 (SD = 6.7). Additionally, normative data provided 
by The University of Sydney (2019) for age > 65 for 
the M-ACE is 27.8 (SD = 1.8), slightly higher than was 
found in our sample (27.00). Our sample with only 4 
participants with a pre-diagnosis of MCI had scored 
lower in MoCA, ACE-III, and M-ACE compared to 
normative data. This supports the view that our sam­
ple may have contained more participants with cogni­
tive impairments than those with a prior diagnosis.
The mean MoCA-MIS score in our study of 12.24 
(SD = 2.97) was similar to the control group mean score 
of 12.2 (SD = 2.8) reported by Kaur et al. (2018), which 
also had a similar mean age 72.7 (SD = 10).
Discussion
Our findings highlight a critical distinction between 
correlation and diagnostic agreement across cognitive 
screening tools. Despite strong score correlations, agree­
ment was often weaker, particularly between the MoCA 
and ACE-III, where only “fair” concordance was 
observed. This discrepancy illustrates the danger of 
assuming that these instruments are interchangeable.
The prevalence of mild cognitive impairment (MCI) 
in individuals aged ≥65 has been estimated at 4–19%, 
depending on how MCI is defined and interpreted 
(Livingston et al., 2017). In our sample, three of 68 
participants aged ≥65 had an existing MCI diagnosis 
(4%), aligning with the lower bound of this range. If the 
upper bound (19%) were applied, our sample could 
potentially have included an additional 10 undiagnosed 
cases. Despite this, across all four measures (MoCA, 
MIS, ACE-III, and M-ACE) 41% of our participants 
fell below the normal cognition threshold in at least 
one test, which is considerably higher than would be 
expected by population prevalence alone. These partici­
pants would be classified as sub-threshold if just one test 
were used in their cognitive assessment.
The accurate identification of MCI and dementia are 
critical, as misclassification can lead to significant psy­
chosocial consequences for patients and caregivers 
(Mirza et al., 2017; Munawar et al., 2023); false positives 
may cause unnecessary anxiety and reduce quality of life 
(Carter et al., 2023; Milby et al., 2017), while false 
negatives delay access to support and early intervention.
Our study used participants from the general com­
munity, whereas earlier studies (e.g., Cheung et al.,  
2015; Matias-Guiu, Cortés-Martínez, et al. 2017; 
Matias-Guiu, Valles-Salgado, et al. 2017 Wang et al.,  
2019) used clinical samples, except one that assessed 
participants in long-term care (Grasina et al., 2024), 
JOURNAL OF CLINICAL AND EXPERIMENTAL NEUROPSYCHOLOGY
9
making direct comparison difficult. It is unknown how 
many of our participants without prior diagnosis were 
falsely identified by these tests as having impaired cog­
nition due to other factors (e.g., fatigue, inattention, 
cognitive profile, or other reasons). Alternatively, some 
of these individuals may genuinely have met the clinical 
criteria for MCI had they been assessed more fully, 
including an informant-supported report of subjective 
cognitive complaints, objective cognitive impairment in 
one or more domains relative to age and education, 
intact daily activities, and the absence of dementia 
(Petersen, 2004).
Although illustrative rather than inferential, in our 
data, two participants with preexisting MCI diagnoses 
scored below the normal threshold on MoCA (scores 24 
and 25) while obtaining very high scores in the ACE-III 
(scores 90 and 96). It may be that MoCA was used to 
make the clinical judgment, which was shared with 
these individuals, but that this conclusion may not 
have been reached had ACE-III rather than MoCA 
been used.
These results illustrate the importance of a clinical 
assessment that takes into consideration demographic 
variables and subjective experience, such as scores in the 
Clinical Dementia Rating (CDR, Morris, 1993) or the 
Multifactorial Memory Questionnaire (MMQ, Troyer & 
Rich, 2002), in addition to raw test scores. This will 
mitigate the observation that test scores may disagree, 
that some tests may be more liberal than others, and that 
some tests focus on cognitive domains that may be 
more/less affected in the individual case or may reflect 
the presence of differences in existing cognitive profile. 
This suggests that premorbid measures of baseline cog­
nitive ability may also be useful in the constellation of 
evidence used to diagnose MCI/dementia, such as read­
ing tests (Bright & van der Linde, 2020; van der Linde & 
Bright, 2024), enabling the clinician to differentiate 
between long-standing individual differences and emer­
ging cognitive decline. Nevertheless, the liberal ten­
dency of MoCA could be advantageous in primary 
care settings where the cost of missing early decline is 
high, but less so in contexts where false positives carry 
significant psychosocial consequences.
The large correlation between the MoCA and ACE- 
III is in line with earlier research (Giebel & Challis,  
2017; Wang et al., 2019). Despite this a discrepancy 
between MoCA and ACE-III is apparent, with twice as 
many participants falling below the normal cognition 
threshold in MoCA compared to ACE-III. This is con­
sistent with earlier studies (e.g., Tsoi et al., 2015) pro­
posing that MoCA has greater sensitivity than ACE-III 
to detect MCI, although potentially at the expense of 
specificity, with greater numbers of false positives 
identified (Luis et al., 2009). There are a number of 
possible explanations for this. First, the MoCA places 
greater emphasis on executive function and attention, 
whereas the ACE-III assesses a broader range of 
domains. A lower score on the MoCA relative to the 
ACE-III might suggest specific difficulties in executive 
functioning or attention. Second, there may be differ­
ences in test sensitivity, such that each test may be more 
sensitive to certain types of cognitive impairment, where 
the discrepancy could indicate that one test is detecting 
subtle deficits that the other is not. Third, differences in 
test conditions, fatigue, or familiarity with test formats 
may contribute, although in this case it is the shorter test 
that elicited a greater number of “fails.”
The difference between these tests was particularly 
striking for the small number of participants that pre­
sented with a prior MCI diagnosis. Of four, only one 
scored under the threshold in both ACE-III and MoCA 
and had much lower scores across all tests, indicative of 
dementia, suggesting agreement between MoCA and 
ACE-III perhaps in more severe cases only. However, 
this is not an ideal situation, given that an important 
role of these screening tools is early detection.
In a comparison with MoCA, it has been suggested 
that the M-ACE is an equally suitable screening tool to 
screen for MCI (Larner, 2017); however, we observed 
some disagreement between these tests. For instance, 10 
participants scored below the normal threshold in the 
MoCA whilst obtaining a M-ACE score in the normal 
range. Conversely, 6 participants scored in the normal 
range in the MoCA, but fell below the normal threshold 
for the M-ACE. What is more interesting, although 
M-ACE scores were derived from the ACE-III scores, 
3 participants scored in the normal range for ACE-III 
but scored below the normal threshold for M-ACE.
In addition to the large correlation between the 
MoCA and ACE-III, a follow-up analysis examined the 
association 
between 
specific 
question 
domains 
(Supplementary Material A), indicating that the highest 
correlations with the MoCA score came from the ACE- 
III attention and language sub-scores. These are the sub- 
domains that contain more than one identical or almost 
identical items with the MoCA, such as assessment date 
and location, the Serial Sevens test (also known as the 
Descending Subtraction Task), picture naming, and 
repeating sentences. This may be responsible for the 
stronger association observed.
Although previous studies have raised concerns 
about the influence of age and education on cognitive 
screening results (Bruno & Schurmann Vignaga, 2019; 
Malek-Ahmadi et al., 2015), our findings showed no 
significant correlation between either factor and total 
or sub-domain scores on any test. This suggests that

these measures are relatively independent of age and 
education, which is beneficial in that it reduces the 
risk of misclassifying healthy aging or lower educational 
attainment as cognitive impairment, an issue previously 
noted in relation to the MoCA (Khaw et al., 2021; 
O’Caoimh et al., 2016) and ACE-III (Jubb & Evans,  
2015; Matias-Guiu et al., 2017; Bruno & Schurmann 
Vignaga, 2019).
MoCA-MIS may be a better measure of memory 
function compared to the MoCA Delayed Recall Task 
alone, and the MoCA-MIS showed a stronger positive 
association with the ACE-III memory sub-domain 
score, which is a more comprehensive assessment of 
the memory abilities compared to the MoCA Delayed 
Recall Task. This is in line with the findings by Kim et al. 
(2021), in which a large correlation between MoCA-MIS 
and SNSB (Seoul Neuropsychological Screening 
Battery) memory domain scores was reported for MCI 
participants. The MoCA-MIS may help differentiate 
whether a memory deficit stems from retrieval or 
encoding difficulties. An inability to exploit provided 
cues may suggest impaired memory encoding. In our 
sample, 12 participants had MoCA scores within the 
normal range coupled with low MoCA-MIS scores. 
These individuals may have been compensating for 
memory difficulties using alternative strategies. 
However, a small number fell below the normal thresh­
old on the M-ACE (n = 1), or on both the M-ACE and 
ACE-III (n = 1). Poor free recall with improved perfor­
mance with cues is typical in MCI, in which cueing 
supports retrieval. In contrast, failure to improve even 
with cues suggests a deficit in encoding or storage, 
which is more characteristic of AD (Clément et al.,  
2010; Hudon et al., 2011).
An observation related to the correlation matrix in 
Supplementary Material A is that MoCA scores correlate 
more highly with memory-related measures (Delayed 
Recall Task and MoCA-MIS) than other measures, sug­
gesting that memory is the most important cognitive 
domain in determining the overall score. This is despite 
the Delayed Recall Task only contributing 17% of the final 
MoCA score, and that only the un-cued recall score from 
MoCA-MIS is used. Similarly, aside from M-ACE, ACE- 
III also correlated most highly with ACE Memory sub- 
score than other cognitive domains, despite contributing 
the same number of points overall as language (26).
Whilst many studies rely on correlation or ROC 
analysis to compare tests, we found only “fair” concor­
dance between MoCA and ACE-III with Cohen’s kappa. 
This suggests that despite high correlation, the tests may 
lead to different clinical outcomes in nearly half of the 
cases, underscoring the importance of not relying on 
single test scores for diagnosis. Giebel and Challis 
(2017) also used Cohen’s kappa and found “poor” to 
“fair” agreement between ACE-III and MoCA to differ­
entiate between mild and moderate dementia (only), 
depending on the threshold used, suggesting that lim­
ited agreement between screeners extends beyond MCI 
to mild and moderate dementia.
This study has several limitations. First, our participant 
cohort, which although similar in size to Hsieh et al. (2013), 
Peixoto et al. (2018), McCarthy et al. (2024), Grasina et al. 
(2024), and Cheung et al. (2015), and larger than Giebel 
and Challis (2017) and Jubb and Evans (2015), was rela­
tively small, and being a community-based sample, had 
relatively few participants with a prior diagnosis of MCI. 
However, MCI is highly underdiagnosed in the community 
with around 92% of expected cases being undiagnosed (Liu 
et al., 2024). The sample also had relatively high educational 
attainment. Second, M-ACE scores were derived from 
ACE-III scores, and this may have decreased the diagnostic 
accuracy of M-ACE scoring as thresholds may have been 
established based on independent administration. Third, 
that there was no objective criterion, such as a full neurop­
sychological assessment. However, as described in the 
Introduction, in the UK (and elsewhere), unlike the 
U.S.A., initial diagnosis following GP referral relies upon 
MoCA, ACE-III, or other validated measure listed in the 
NICE guidelines, and a “gold standard” neuropsychologi­
cal assessment is not typically used. However, future work 
should evaluate these findings using clinical groups (viz., 
equal sized groups of participants with MCI and dementia 
diagnoses) in addition to a sample confirmed to be neuro­
logically healthy through a comprehensive neuropsycholo­
gical assessment.
Currently, no National Institute for Health and Care 
Excellence (NICE) guidance for assessing MCI in the UK 
exists (Dunne et al., 2021). In a surveillance proposal 
(National Institute for Health and Care Excellence 
[NICE], 2023) considering recommendations for MCI 
and extending the existing guidance on dementia 
(NICE, 2018), it was concluded that MCI is a complex 
and heterogeneous syndrome and not all MCI cases are 
related to dementia. Furthermore, research has yet to 
provide robust evidence to develop MCI guidelines, 
with the lack of quality publications specifically high­
lighted in this report. In the absence of NICE guidance, 
this study contributes evidence on the community- 
prevalence of those not reaching the normal threshold 
in MoCA, MoCA-MIS, ACE-III, and M-ACE, and the 
degree of test concordance.
Conclusion
Our community-based sample likely better represents 
individuals at earlier stages of cognitive decline, 
JOURNAL OF CLINICAL AND EXPERIMENTAL NEUROPSYCHOLOGY
11
including undiagnosed MCI, compared to prior studies 
that use participants with confirmed diagnoses for com­
parison against participants confirmed to be neurologi­
cally healthy. Unlike previous work that classified 
below-threshold scores without a diagnosis as “false 
positives,” we remained agnostic, reflecting the preva­
lence of undiagnosed MCI. Administering MoCA, 
ACE-III, MoCA-MIS, and M-ACE to the same partici­
pants allowed us to assess diagnostic agreement using 
standard thresholds, complemented by inter-rater relia­
bility and correlation metrics, providing novel insights 
into test concordance.
Notably, a large proportion of participants scored below 
threshold in at least one test, exceeding normative expecta­
tions. These findings highlight the complexity of defining 
normal cognition in community samples and underscore 
the importance of considering demographics, subjective 
reports, and premorbid function alongside screening 
scores. Our findings are particularly relevant in countries 
like the UK, in which screening tests are frequently used 
for initial dementia diagnosis, in conjunction with self- 
report and informant history, rather than a fuller diagnos­
tic battery. As demand for effective cognitive assessment 
grows, including digital tools (Modarres et al., 2023), care­
ful evaluation of existing instruments is crucial to optimize 
the design of future screening approaches.
